Name: spectral_precision_routing
Title: Spectral Precision Routing: A Training-Free Framework for Adaptive Numerical Precision Allocation
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that the local covariance condition number and spectral decay rate of hidden states serve as direct proxies for quantization sensitivity, enabling training-free, adaptive numerical precision allocation in neural networks.
Related Work: Recent works such as Spectral Latent Steering and Spectral Quantization Routing have explored the use of spectral methods for training-free optimization and precision allocation. However, these methods rely on static or heuristic approaches to determine precision levels. Our proposal distinguishes from existing literature by introducing a dynamic, geometry-aware precision routing framework that adapts to changing input distributions and network conditions.
Abstract: We propose Spectral Precision Routing (SPR), a training-free framework that dynamically allocates numerical precision in neural networks based on real-time activation geometry. SPR computes local covariance condition numbers and spectral decay rates of hidden states, mapping them to a precision-switching policy that enforces strict latency budgets. We theoretically ground the routing mechanism with PAC-Bayes bounds that link spectral conditioning to generalization stability under quantization noise. Evaluated on single-GPU setups across diverse benchmarks, SPR achieves a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier compared to static and heuristic mixed-precision baselines.
Experiments: Evaluate SPR on WILDS benchmarks with varying input distributions and network architectures
Compare SPR with state-of-the-art mixed-precision methods, including static and heuristic approaches
Investigate the impact of spectral conditioning on generalization stability under quantization noise
Assess the effectiveness of SPR in enforcing strict latency budgets and reducing computational overhead
Risk Factors and Limitations: The proposed framework may require careful calibration of spectral diagnostics and precision-switching policies
The use of real-time activation geometry may introduce additional computational overhead
The effectiveness of SPR may depend on the specific network architecture and task
